---
title: "Course Data README"
format: html
editor: visual
---

::: {.callout-warning}
This README is a draft for instructor review and updates before teaching. It is
based on repository inspection on 2026-07-07. Confirm source, permissions,
provenance, and any sensitive-data language before release.
:::

# Recommended default for Exercises 1 and 2

## QCRC COVID-19 ICU workbook

- File: `data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx`
- Recommended use: Exercises 1 and 2 mini-manuscript/report arc.
- Why use it: It is rich enough for descriptive statistics, tests, logistic
  regression, repeated sheet inspection, formula syntax, and polished Quarto
  reporting.
- Main sheet for most students: `Main_Dataset`.
- Other sheets available for advanced students: `Oxygen_Delivery`, `SOFA`,
  `Long_Labs`, `Long_Vitals`, `Static_Compliance`, `PAO2_FIO2`, `ICU_Meds`,
  `Intake_Form`, and `Daily_Form`.
- Useful main-sheet variables include age, sex, race, mortality endpoints,
  ICU length of stay, BMI, treatment indicator, intubation, CRRT, labs, and
  oxygenation measures.
- Important caution: Some numeric variables are stored as text, and missing
  values may be written as `"."`. Clean and document variables before modeling.

# Other course datasets

Students may use another dataset if they have a question that genuinely fits it
better than QCRC. They should still produce the same report deliverables.

## Measles vaccination and case data

- File: `data/measles_final.Rds`
- Structure: country-year records with measles cases, population, MCV1/MCV2
  coverage, country, region, and sub-region.
- Good for: iteration, functions, functional programming, trend summaries,
  regional comparisons, and Poisson-style modeling.
- Less ideal for Exercises 1 and 2: It can become a larger ecological analysis
  quickly, and students may spend more time defining denominators than polishing
  a compact manuscript.

## Simulated city cases

- File: `data/city-cases.csv`
- Structure: monthly city-level records with region, industry, date, sex,
  exposure-like values, population, temperature, and cases.
- Good for: aggregation, plotting, rates, and disease-mapping-adjacent practice.
- Less ideal for the default report: It is simulated and needs more framing
  before it feels like a manuscript.

## SIR observed data

- File: `data/SIR-observed.csv`
- Structure: daily total cases.
- Good for: ODE and epidemic-curve exercises.
- Less ideal for Exercises 1 and 2: It is intentionally small and does not
  support many covariate or manuscript-style choices.

## Mosquito surveillance data

- File: `data/mos-nyc.csv`
- Structure: county-level PCR-positive and pool-submission counts.
- Good for: binomial proportions, mapping, surveillance summaries, and public
  health communication.
- Less ideal for the default report: It is narrower than QCRC and may require
  spatial context that belongs more naturally with the Day 3 GIS module.

# Recommendation for students

Use QCRC unless one of the other datasets clearly matches your professional
interests. If you switch datasets, add two sentences to your report:

1. Why this dataset is better for your question.
1. What limitation you accept by not using QCRC.
